prompt:
Our deploy tool keeps a JSON config per environment and people keep hand-editing staging and prod off the same base until neither looks like the other. I want a proper three-way merge library so the tool can reconcile them like git would, but structure-aware instead of line-based. TypeScript, bare node (tests run with node --test, type stripping, zero npm deps). Three modules, and please keep them separate — merge logic, report rendering, and resolution are different concerns:

merge.ts — export merge3(base, ours, theirs, options?) -> { merged, conflicts }. Values are anything JSON (null is a real value, never a deletion — deletion is the key being absent). Rules, in order: if ours and theirs are deep-equal, take it; if only one side changed from base, take that side (including deletions); if both changed and both are plain objects, recurse key by key (a non-object base just means everything inside is an addition); arrays get the strategy treatment below; anything else is a conflict. Conflict records: { path, kind, base?, ours?, theirs? } — dotted path ('' for the root), kind one of 'edit-edit' (base existed, both edited), 'delete-edit' (base existed, one side deleted, other edited), 'add-add' (no base, both added differently); the base/ours/theirs keys are present ONLY for sides that have a value. On conflict, merged keeps the base value (or omits the key if base had none). conflicts come out in traversal order — base's keys, then keys only ours added, then keys only theirs added, depth-first. merged must be a fresh structure, never aliasing the inputs.

Array strategies: options.arrays is 'atomic' (default), 'union', 'ours' or 'theirs'; options.arrayOverrides maps a dotted path to a strategy for that exact path, beating the global. Strategies only kick in when BOTH sides are arrays and BOTH actually changed — a lone edit always wins wholesale, and array-vs-non-array falls back to an atomic conflict. atomic: deep-equality or conflict. union: every element of ours in order, then elements of theirs not already present (deep equality dedupe). ours/theirs: take that side. Unknown strategy anywhere -> Error naming the bad strategy.

markers.ts — export renderConflicts(result) -> string. Empty string for a clean merge. Per conflict, a diff3-flavored block:

# <path>: <kind>
<<<<<<< ours
<value>
||||||| base
<value>
=======
<value>
>>>>>>> theirs

where <value> is JSON.stringify(v, null, 2) or the literal (missing) for an absent side, the root path prints as (root), blocks are joined by one blank line, and the whole report ends with a newline.

resolve.ts — export resolve(result, choices) -> new { merged, conflicts }. choices maps a conflict path to 'ours' | 'theirs' | 'base'; picking a side that has no value deletes the key; the root resolves through the '' path. Resolved conflicts disappear from the list, unresolved ones stay, the input result is not mutated. A path that isn't a pending conflict, or a choice that isn't one of the three -> Error naming it.

The acceptance tests are the spec of record: test_merge.ts, test_arrays.ts, test_report.ts — run with node --test test_merge.ts test_arrays.ts test_report.ts and leave them alone.
